skill.name: mapreduce
skill.description: Coordinate parallel planning, implementation, and debugging tasks across multiple AI agents or provider CLIs, then consolidate the results into one documented outcome.
skill.summary: Runs MapReduce-style AI workflows for planning, coding, debugging, and synthesis.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command-like patterns, but most are Markdown examples for MapReduce workflows rather than executable skill code. The weak cryptography detections are false positives from scoring rubrics and Markdown text. The remaining risk is medium because the skill instructs agents to run external AI CLIs, write project files, and optionally delete generated artifacts.

medium_findings.title: External AI CLI Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Bash commands that invoke Codex, Gemini, Cursor, and OpenCode. These commands may send project prompts or specifications to external providers and run with local agent permissions. This appears intentional and documented, not malicious.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly show external provider CLI commands. The surrounding context presents them as optional workflow examples, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Orchestrated File Writes and Consolidation
medium_findings.description: The skill coordinates workers through project files and instructs reducers to read and write planning, implementation, and diagnosis artifacts. This is core functionality, but it can overwrite important files if paths are chosen poorly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file conventions and reducer prompts directly describe writing outputs into the repository. The risk depends on user-selected paths, so it is moderate and manageable.

low_findings.title: Scoped Destructive Cleanup Example
low_findings.description: The reference includes an optional cleanup command that deletes generated planner files. It is scoped to a conventional artifact path, but users could lose intermediate evidence if they run it prematurely.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The rm command is clearly present and scoped to generated files. It is documented as optional cleanup, so it is a low operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Provider Prompts
dangerous_patterns.description: Some examples assemble provider prompts with shell substitution from local files. This can be safe for trusted files, but it may expose repository content to external providers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is visible in Bash examples. The files read are local project artifacts, so the concern is data disclosure and shell adaptation risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Full-Auto External Agent Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation shows Codex running with full-auto style permissions. Users should restrict this mode to trusted repositories and review generated changes before applying them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The full-auto flag appears in command examples. The surrounding text shows normal orchestration use, but the permission level increases operational risk.

content.value_statement: Complex planning, coding, and debugging tasks often benefit from several independent AI perspectives. This skill provides a MapReduce workflow to fan out work, collect results, and consolidate the best outcome.
actual_capabilities: Defines a MapReduce workflow with map, collect, and reduce phases.
Shows how to launch parallel planners with different strategic biases.
Documents optional provider CLI patterns for Codex, Gemini, Cursor, and OpenCode.
Provides conventions for intermediate planning, implementation, debug, and review files.
Includes rubrics for consolidating plans, code implementations, and bug fixes.
Explains timeout handling, attribution, conflict resolution, and artifact preservation.
limitations: It is documentation and workflow guidance, not a standalone execution engine.
External provider commands require installed CLIs and configured credentials.
Reducers depend on available agents that are referenced but not included here.
Users must review generated code and plans before applying them.

use_cases.title: Plan a Complex Feature
use_cases.description: Run conservative, aggressive, security, and scalability planning passes, then merge them into one roadmap.

use_cases.title: Compare Code Implementations
use_cases.description: Generate multiple implementations from the same specification and select the strongest result using tests and a scoring rubric.

use_cases.title: Resolve Difficult Bugs
use_cases.description: Collect independent diagnoses, compare evidence, and consolidate a fix with clear attribution and verification notes.

prompt_templates.title: Create Planning Perspectives
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mapreduce skill to plan this feature from three perspectives: conservative, security-focused, and scalability-focused. Save each plan separately and consolidate them into one roadmap.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Implementations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mapreduce skill to generate two implementations for this specification. Run available tests, compare quality, and recommend the implementation to keep.

prompt_templates.title: Build Debug Consensus
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mapreduce skill to collect independent bug diagnoses. Compare root causes, verify proposed fixes, and produce one resolution report with confidence levels.

prompt_templates.title: Customize Reduction Criteria
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mapreduce skill with a custom reducer priority. Weight security and maintainability above speed, preserve attribution, and document every conflict resolution decision.

output_examples.input: A team needs an implementation plan for user authentication.
output: A consolidated roadmap with phases, risks, and exit criteria.
An attribution table showing which planner contributed each decision.
A conflict summary for disputed approaches, such as session storage.

output_examples.input: A developer wants to compare three generated service implementations.
output: A comparison report with test results, quality scores, and known defects.
A selected implementation recommendation with required follow-up fixes.
Notes about traits worth keeping from non-selected versions.

output_examples.input: A maintainer has a bug with several possible root causes.
output: A verified diagnosis that explains the most likely root cause.
A minimal fix recommendation with safety and regression notes.
A confidence rating based on agreement between independent analyses.

best_practices: Use clear specifications so every worker solves the same problem.
Preserve intermediate outputs until the final result has been reviewed.
Set explicit reducer priorities for security, correctness, and maintainability.
anti_patterns: Do not run external provider CLIs on sensitive repositories without approval.
Do not merge generated code before tests and human review pass.
Do not delete intermediate artifacts before attribution and audit needs are complete.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow instructions and command examples for agents or users to run deliberately.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention external commands?
faq.answer: The documentation includes examples for Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, local validation, git, and cleanup commands.

faq.question: Can I use this without external AI providers?
faq.answer: Yes. The main pattern also works with local subagents and file-based consolidation.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing outputs?
faq.answer: Review generated plans, code, tests, attribution, and any file paths that reducers may overwrite.

faq.question: Is this suitable for sensitive codebases?
faq.answer: Use caution. Disable external provider steps unless your organization approves those tools for the repository.

faq.question: What makes MapReduce useful for AI work?
faq.answer: It separates independent generation from synthesis, which can expose trade-offs and reduce single-model blind spots.
